🇬🇧 EN

ECB SAFE Survey — Vlna 38

Slovensko · Eurozóna · Nemecko  |  Vygenerované 30 Jun 2026

Survey questions collected on a 3-month basis (14 questions)
TopicIDQuestionModule

Július Koller — Pre každú príležitosť... osviežujúci národný podnik. (UFO) (1978)

Zhrnutie

  • Slovak firms reported a net tightening in both interest rates and non-interest financing costs, with non-interest costs deteriorating sharply and remaining above euro-area averages, while collateral and other terms showed modest tightening.
  • The financing gap for credit lines widened dramatically to +14.8pp—driven almost entirely by surging demand—leaving Slovakia more stressed than the euro area, despite easing conditions in bank loans where availability still slightly exceeded need.
  • Discouragement from applying for bank loans more than doubled in Slovakia, with application rates falling well below the euro area, indicating growing self-censorship and withdrawal from credit markets despite low rejection rates.
  • Firms expect bank loan availability to deteriorate further, with Slovak pessimism exceeding the euro area, and trade credit expectations showing the sharpest divergence—signalling idiosyncratic domestic financing stress.
  • Slovak firms face a sharp economic squeeze: turnover and profits declined more sharply than in the euro area, while labour costs rose far above the euro-area average, intensifying margin pressures.
  • Turnover expectations plummeted to the weakest on record relative to the euro area, though firms remained relatively more optimistic about fixed investment, suggesting a shift away from current operations toward future capacity.

Obsah

  • Podmienky financovania
    • Slovak firms report net tightening in interest rates, reversing prior easing
    • Slovak credit line stress surged as demand spike overshadowed easy bank loans
    • Slovak firms' financing gap unclear as surging discouragement masks credit demand
    • Slovak firms expect bank loan availability to deteriorate, widening gap with EA
    • Slovak firms rely heavily on working capital and new product financing
    • Slovak banks remain willing to lend but macro and public-support conditions deteriorate further
  • Ekonomická situácia firiem
    • Slovak firms report sharply falling turnover and deepening profit squeeze
    • Slovak firms' turnover optimism plunges well below euro-area peers
    • Slovak firms expect selling prices and wages to rise faster than euro-area peers
    • Slovak firms see stronger upside inflation risks than euro-area peers
    • Slovak firms rate access to finance least pressing, below EA average

Podmienky financovania

Slovak firms report net tightening in interest rates, reversing prior easing

Changes in Terms and Conditions of Bank Financing (Q10)

  • A net 5.5% of Slovak firms (n=62) reported tighter interest rate conditions in wave 38, reversing the net easing of -8.3% in the previous wave, though remaining well below EA (+27.3%) and DE (+37.5%).
  • A net 40.6% of Slovak firms (n=60) reported tighter non-interest financing costs (charges, fees, commissions), broadly in line with DE (+48.7%) and above the EA average (+36.6%), compared with a net 42.4% in the previous wave.
  • A net 6.1% of Slovak firms (n=62) reported adverse conditions on available loan size, a slight improvement from +7.0% in the previous wave, while collateral requirements (+1.4%) and other terms (+8.0%) — both previously unavailable — now show modest net tightening.

* Túto otázku dostávajú iba firmy, ktoré v minulosti využili alebo žiadali o daný typ financovania. Nižší počet respondentov oproti celkovej vzorke je zámerný a neindikuje problém s kvalitou dát — pozri metodológiu ECB SAFE.

Slovak credit line stress surged as demand spike overshadowed easy bank loans

Financing Need vs Availability Gap (Q5/Q9)

  • For bank loans, Slovak firms (n=254) reported a financing gap of -4.0pp — availability (+1.4pp) exceeded need (-2.6pp) — remaining far less stressed than the EA gap of +1.7pp, though the gap widened slightly from -7.6pp as loan demand partially recovered.
  • The credit line gap for Slovak firms (n=278) surged to +14.8pp from +4.8pp in the prior wave, exceeding the EA gap of +12.6pp and making Slovakia more stressed than the euro area; the deterioration was almost entirely demand-driven, with need jumping +9.2pp to +12.3pp while availability was nearly flat at -2.5pp — in sharp contrast to the easing seen in bank loans.
  • For trade credit, Slovak firms (n=150) recorded a financing gap of +6.3pp, down from +9.8pp, as need fell to +7.1pp from +10.1pp, flipping Slovakia from more stressed than the EA (gap +8.2pp) to less stressed than it for this instrument.

* Túto otázku dostávajú iba firmy, ktoré v minulosti využili alebo žiadali o daný typ financovania. Nižší počet respondentov oproti celkovej vzorke je zámerný a neindikuje problém s kvalitou dát — pozri metodológiu ECB SAFE.

Slovak firms' financing gap unclear as surging discouragement masks credit demand

Bank Loan Applications and Access Conditions (Q7A/Q7B)

  • In wave 38, Slovakia's financing gap (n=149) could not be computed, but discouragement_rate surged to 5.76% — more than double the EA rate of 2.74% — signalling sharp self-censorship in credit demand.
  • Slovakia's application rate fell to 19.18% in wave 38, well below the EA's 34.12%, compared with 23.51% vs 35.93% in the prior wave, suggesting Slovak firms are increasingly withdrawing from the credit market before applying.
  • In wave 37, Slovakia's financing gap of 3.58% (n=147) was below the EA's 5.02%, driven by a low rejection rate of 5.31% (vs EA 5.00%), indicating the wave 38 spike in discouragement — not supply-side tightening — is the primary concern.

* Túto otázku dostávajú iba firmy, ktoré v minulosti využili alebo žiadali o daný typ financovania. Nižší počet respondentov oproti celkovej vzorke je zámerný a neindikuje problém s kvalitou dát — pozri metodológiu ECB SAFE.

🤖 Táto sekcia obsahuje dáta získané AI agentom priamym dopytovaním databázy SAFE počas generovania správy.

Slovak firms expect bank loan availability to deteriorate, widening gap with EA

Expected Availability of External Financing (Q23)

  • A net -5.16% of Slovak firms (n=130) expected bank loan availability to deteriorate in wave 38, worsening from a net -1.93% in the previous quarter, signalling accelerating pessimism.
  • The EA-wide net balance for bank loan availability also turned more negative (-3.80%, n=2948) from near-neutral -0.45% in wave 37, yet Slovak deterioration expectations now exceed the EA average, suggesting local supply tightness beyond the broader euro-area trend.
  • Slovak firms reported the sharpest divergence in trade credit availability expectations (net -10.22%, n=79) versus EA peers (net -0.87%, n=1912), indicating idiosyncratic domestic financing stress not yet reflected in euro-area-wide data.

* Túto otázku dostávajú iba firmy, ktoré v minulosti využili alebo žiadali o daný typ financovania. Nižší počet respondentov oproti celkovej vzorke je zámerný a neindikuje problém s kvalitou dát — pozri metodológiu ECB SAFE.

† Bunky s menej ako 10 platnými odpoveďami v danej kombinácii vlny × krajiny × položky sú vynechané z grafu; medzery v sérii indikujú nedostatok dát pre dané obdobie.

Slovak firms rely heavily on working capital and new product financing

Purpose of Financing (Q6A)

  • 55.7% of Slovak firms (n=229) cited inventory and working capital as a financing purpose, far exceeding the EA average of 39.5%, and broadly unchanged from 55.8% in the previous wave.
  • 32.8% of Slovak firms (n=227) cited developing and launching new products or services, notably above the EA average of 20.2% and up from 29.9% in the previous quarter.
  • Only 34.2% of Slovak firms (n=229) cited fixed investment as a financing purpose, below both the EA average of 36.8% and the prior wave reading of 40.3%, suggesting a shift away from capital expenditure.

Slovak banks remain willing to lend but macro and public-support conditions deteriorate further

Factors Affecting Access to External Financing (Q11)

  • A net 7.8% of Slovak firms (n=306) reported improved willingness of banks to provide credit in wave 38, remaining positive but easing from a net 10.4% in the previous wave, while the EA held broadly stable at a net +5.5% (n=6,570).
  • A net 44.4% of Slovak firms (n=442) reported a deterioration in the general economic outlook, worsening from a net -38.3% in wave 37 and significantly exceeding the EA average of -26.1% (n=10,420), signalling a sharper macro-level drag on financing conditions.
  • Access to public financial support deteriorated markedly for Slovak firms, with a net -33.8% (n=298) in wave 38 compared with a net -25.0% in wave 37, more than double the EA-wide reading of -15.5% (n=7,054).

Ekonomická situácia firiem

Slovak firms report sharply falling turnover and deepening profit squeeze

Business Situation Indicators (Q2)

  • A net -26% of Slovak firms (n=229) reported declining turnover in wave 38, worsening markedly compared with a net -15% in the previous quarter, while the EA recorded a near-flat net +1%.
  • A net -40% of Slovak firms (n=229) reported falling profits — more than double the EA net balance of -17% — compared with a net -22% for Slovakia in wave 37, signalling an intensifying squeeze.
  • Labour cost pressures remained far above the EA average, with a net 84% of Slovak firms (n=229) reporting rising labour costs versus a net 63% for the EA, up from net 62% and 50% respectively in the prior wave.

Slovak firms' turnover optimism plunges well below euro-area peers

Expected Changes in Turnover and Investment (Q26)

  • A net 6.75% of Slovak firms (n=228) expected turnover to increase, sharply below the euro-area net of +25.88% (n=5,266) in wave 38 — the widest SK–EA gap on record since wave 30.
  • Slovak turnover expectations fell dramatically compared with a net +20.90% in the previous wave (wave 36, n=169), a drop of roughly 14 percentage points against a broadly stable EA reading of +25.88%.
  • By contrast, Slovak firms remained more upbeat on fixed investment, with a net +14.67% expecting increases — outpacing the EA net of +9.45% and reversing the prior-wave pattern where SK (+10.79%) lagged EA (+12.58%).

🤖 Táto sekcia obsahuje dáta získané AI agentom priamym dopytovaním databázy SAFE počas generovania správy.

Slovak firms expect selling prices and wages to rise faster than euro-area peers

Price, Wage and Employment Expectations (Q31/Q34)

  • A net mean of +4.27% selling price growth was expected by Slovak firms (n=418) in wave 38, exceeding the EA average of +3.63% and up from +4.63% in wave 37, signalling persistent price pressures.
  • Slovak firms anticipate +4.21% wage growth alongside +8.31% non-labour input price rises (n=417), compared with EA averages of +2.80% and +5.84% respectively, flagging acute margin compression risk.
  • A net 83.62% of Slovak firms (n=229) reported rising labour costs in wave 38 — up sharply from a net 61.84% in wave 37 and well above the EA net of 62.65% — confirming that wage cost pressures are already materialising.

† Bunky s menej ako 10 platnými odpoveďami v danej kombinácii vlny × krajiny × položky sú vynechané z grafu; medzery v sérii indikujú nedostatok dát pre dané obdobie.

Slovak firms see stronger upside inflation risks than euro-area peers

Inflation Risk Outlook (Q33)

  • A net 58.93% of Slovak firms (n=229) reported upside inflation risks in wave 38, exceeding the euro-area net balance of +55.32% (n=5,278) and up from a net 53.13% in wave 37.
  • Slovak firms' expected selling price increases averaged 4.27% in wave 38, well above the EA average of 3.63%, while input price expectations remained exceptionally elevated at 8.31% versus 5.84% for the EA — consistent with local price persistence.
  • Five-year inflation expectations among Slovak firms rose to 8.57% in wave 38 compared with 7.06% in wave 37, diverging sharply from the EA five-year mean of 6.28%, signalling that Slovak firms anticipate more persistent long-run price pressures.

† Bunky s menej ako 10 platnými odpoveďami v danej kombinácii vlny × krajiny × položky sú vynechané z grafu; medzery v sérii indikujú nedostatok dát pre dané obdobie.

🤖 Táto sekcia obsahuje dáta získané AI agentom priamym dopytovaním databázy SAFE počas generovania správy.

Slovak firms rate access to finance least pressing, below EA average

Most Pressing Business Problems (Q0B)

  • Slovak firms rated access to finance at 3.91 out of 10 (n=223) in wave 38, slightly below the EA average of 3.99 and the lowest-scoring problem in Slovakia, suggesting finance constraints are not a primary concern.
  • The access-to-finance score for Slovakia edged up from 3.72 in wave 37 to 3.91 in wave 38, narrowing the gap with the EA (4.14 in wave 37 vs. 3.99 in wave 38), indicating SK firms' perceptions are converging toward the euro-area norm.
  • By contrast, the most pressing problem for Slovak firms in wave 38 was regulation (6.18 out of 10, n=225), followed closely by costs of production or labour (6.19, n=228), both far outweighing access-to-finance concerns.

Zdroj: ECB SAFE mikrodáta. Čistá bilancia = % podnikov hlásiacich nárast mínus % podnikov hlásiacich pokles. Kladná hodnota = sprísnenie / rast (nepriaznivé pre firmy, ak nie je uvedené inak). Záporná hodnota = uvoľnenie / pokles.
